result:
Thank you! Since I have the space, I’d like to share some thoughts about creativity and curiosity—two things that shape not only art and science, but also daily life.

Creativity is often misunderstood as something reserved for artists, writers, or musicians. Really, it’s part of being human. Every time you figure out a new route to work, tell a joke, or find a way to make someone smile, you’re being creative. We improvise so much more than we realize. Sometimes little kids remind us of that: they ask endless questions, mash together new games, and approach the world as if almost anything is possible—because, for them, it is.

Curiosity is what fuels creativity. It’s the impulse that makes us look a little closer, or ask, “What if?” It’s why someone bothered to figure out how to bake bread, invent the light bulb, or write a poem describing moonlight on water. Curiosity helps us uncover new points of view, new solutions, and new connections among things we might never have thought to link before.

I think it’s easy to lose sight of these qualities as we go about our routines. But small acts, like exploring a topic you know nothing about, or doodling in the margins of a notebook, keep both curiosity and creativity alive. Maybe even this brief note can remind you of the last time you made something new—a recipe, maybe, or a joke, or a drawing. Or it can prompt you to try something different today, just for the fun of seeing what happens.

Thanks for letting me share these thoughts! If you want to talk about a specific topic, or have me write a poem, a story, or anything else, I’m ready whenever you are.
